event: The Crucifixion of Jesus Christ on Golgotha with the two thieves
symbols: central cross of Christ elevated above the thieves' crosses — the unique and supreme sacrifice, the Lamb of God lifted up to draw all to himself
darkened turbulent sky — the cosmic darkness that fell from the sixth to the ninth hour, creation convulsing at the death of its Creator
two thieves on either side — the division of humanity at the cross, one repenting and receiving paradise, the other hardening his heart
figure on ladder reaching toward a crucified man — the offering of the vinegar sponge, fulfilling 'for my thirst they gave me vinegar'
mourning women at the foot of the cross — the faithful remnant who stayed when the disciples fled, fulfilling Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce Mary's soul
faint light breaking through clouds at horizon — the promise of resurrection persisting even in the darkest hour
description: Three crosses dominate the composition against a brooding, cloud-laden sky. Christ hangs on the central cross, his pale body illuminated against the darkness. The two thieves are crucified on crosses angled to left and right. A man on a ladder reaches toward one of the crucified. At the base, mourning women including Mary and Mary Magdalene huddle in grief at lower left in dark robes. The landscape of Golgotha is barren and rocky. The palette is overwhelmingly dark -- deep browns, blacks, and greys -- with dramatic shafts of pale light catching Christ's body and cloud edges.
theological_description: Tintoretto's Crucifixion presents the central event of salvation history with raw intensity. The darkened sky is theological: 'from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour' (Matthew 27:45), signifying the powers of sin and death making their supreme assault upon the Light of the World. Yet precisely in this darkness, God's victory is accomplished. The three crosses form the theological architecture: Christ in the center is not one criminal among three but the sinless Lamb of God taking away the sins of the world (John 1:29), 'numbered with the transgressors' in fulfillment of Isaiah 53:12. The two thieves represent humanity's universal condition at the cross: one repented and heard 'Today you will be with me in Paradise' (Luke 23:43), the other hardened his heart. The mourning women embody faithful love under unbearable suffering. Mary experiences Simeon's prophecy: 'a sword will pierce through your own soul' (Luke 2:35). The angular composition conveys cosmic upheaval: 'the curtain of the temple was torn in two, and the earth shook, and the rocks were split' (Matthew 27:51). The old order is destroyed and a new creation inaugurated through the blood of the cross.

scripture_refs.reason: The full account of the Crucifixion at Golgotha -- the principal narrative source for the scene
The Johannine Crucifixion account including Mary at the cross and Christ's words 'It is finished'
The Suffering Servant pierced for our transgressions, numbered with transgressors -- fulfilled in Christ's crucifixion
The penitent and impenitent thieves flanking Christ, and the promise of Paradise
The messianic psalm of suffering Christ quoted from the cross, describing crucifixion in prophetic detail
A sword will pierce your own soul -- Simeon's prophecy fulfilled in Mary's grief at the cross
When I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself -- Christ lifted on the cross as the focal point of humanity's decision
Darkness over all the land from the sixth to the ninth hour -- the supernatural darkness in the brooding sky
